author: @Lio-Chocho
text: Can someone please send me link to Steve´s youtube please? I really want to see some progress with getting electric energy from EZ water but can´t find his youtube site or anything else on internet about it. Thank you very much!

author: @juanmf
text:
Any news or follow up?
Bought the book, I put it to test, considering: hydrophilic surface creates the stack of EZ layers. made a rack of microscope cover glasses 0.13mm thick separated by de-phased ones to make 0.13mm gaps, (ideally EZ-water forming between sheets and flow should "filter/purify" water). But didn't observe ANY exclusion zone at all. Random particles would just fall to the outer glass. Food dye didn't react at all. This glass is hydrophilic AF BTW, water droplets extend to max over it... So I'm confused. hints/thoughts on home made EZ?
Although I did observe before, that small (~1-0.5mm diameter) air bubbles after shaking the water do project a falling tubular structure that reflect light a bit differently, probably thin as hair though... So I'm not sure why my glass experiment failed miserably.

replies.author: @Lio-Chocho
replies.text: Hello. Do you have any new information about getting electric energy from EZ and Bulk water please?

replies.author: @juanmf
replies.text:
​@Lio-Chocho no news on EZ, recently bought a nafion sheet 2"x2" that's what Pollack used, but haven't tested it yet. I did do some Schauberger inspired tests tho. reddit r/SacredGeometry

title: Vortex (Schauberger style) effect on water.
body: Massive difference in a pair of sets water lenses (1, 2, 3 drops from same syringe). One tap water, the other same water after three passes through hyperbolic funnel for tight vortexing+mild magnets ).

The lense effect shows different curvature and the perimeter line also shows lack of symmetry in the tap water. Possibly all attributable to changes in surface tension.

Vortex water wets more. Should make a difference in hydration and plant growth.

Right column is vortexed.

Funnels are generational improvements, used the tallest. Designed to minimize air core diameter. Currently it has 3 ceramic ring magnets flushed at outlet hole. Water retention is somewhere in 10-15 seconds.
---
you can google it, yt deletes comments with links. I have to post an update for that one at some point..

author: @kevin8225
text: If you guys have done more experiments it would be interesting to see. Not sure what temperature you used in the experiment but I believe light and temperature are both important factors in getting EZ water to form. I am surprised more has not been done with EZ water yet. Pollack says when the EZ water forms, the difference in charge and stickiness of the EZ water causes the water in the center to move on its own. So as long as light and temperature are correct, its basically perpetual motion.

author: @martinbesta459
text: Thank you for experiment, but please, I want to ask you, what role the zinc plate plays in your experiment? And I think, there can be error with electrodes placed like this. Because multimeter uses current to measure resistance and when there is current flow through water between outside electrodes, it produces step voltage between those electrodes in water. And when this step voltage is applied to inside electrodes, it can produce some current flow through another multimeter's internal resistance and bring error to both measurements.

author: @MongrelShark
text:
Those two meters are almost certainly interfering with each other. I have no idea what effect causes it, but have observed current from one floating supply piggybacking on another otherwise isolated supply in water.  You cant take two ohms readings in common electrolyte simultaneously. It does not give accurate reading. I've tried and tried. Spent about two weeks playing with water resistors for a big joule thief ages ago (SWES BAC in my SWES playlist there are a few videos with the water resistor). Just getting an accurate ohms reading from water is hard enough without putting two current sources in the water.... Have you noticed how much the resistance changes if you change the current or voltage.... I bet those two meters have differing output values...

Suggested improvement for the test. Use two identical meters and only have one turned on at a time. A Switching circuit could be run from a small micro like an arduino or with logic gates, but keep t slow. No faster than 2-4 hz. Slower would be better 6 times a min?. The water stores charge. ie see my taser water video. I found that out accidentally when I got hit by 40ish kv from a plastic cup of water near my HV rig, about a week after it was last switched on. Plastic cups self discharge 40kv in about 10 min without the water. But add water and make the water side of the cup pos and it'll store many many volts with little loss for months. Won't work with negative water though. I assume its the 2 hydrogen vs 1oxygen..
Works on PET bottles too for the evil prankster... I wouldn't go over about 20KV on a large PET bottle though, Its a fair amount of Joules and may kill someone. Its different to a Leyden jar, as no added salt/electrolyte, no conductors and it holds charge longer...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @Spirit532
replies.text:
I really agree with this top comment.
The entire experiment is somewhat incompetently done, although A for effort, since you didn't blindly believe in someone's words.

Another thing you forget entirely is that there is a PLATE OF ZINC that is CONDUCTIVE. That means the current will flow from the copper, through the water, through zinc, up the water, and up into the other piece of copper, thus destroying any empirical data you have.

This experiment is entirely flawed - you need to do this chemically, not electrically.
Make the cell, collect some of the proverbial magic water, and compare the concentration of Na ions in solution from both samples. Now THAT can be done resistively, with one meter, in separate vials.

It looks like it's promising on the top, but you need to dig a lot further if you want to at least check if you have anything worthy, though I don't think you do.

author: @joeglory425
text: I'm just thinking out loud...if you have a barrier to ions surrounding a sheet of zinc and you want to electroplate zinc out of solution onto the zinc plate, then those zinc ions have to first bust through the EZ layer. I picture that when this happens (a hole in the barrier has been formed) then it allows more zinc ions in but just at the one place where there is a hole in the EZ layer which results in the dreaded formation of dendrites. A test of this hypothesis would be comparing EZ layer thickness with a variety of metals using electrolytes of interest in plating and seeing if there is a correlation between dendrite formation and EZ layer thickness. A further test could involve electrolyte additives known to improve plating quality and checking to see if they correspond to thinner EZ layer thickness. I give consent to anyone out there wanting to work on this idea.
